title: BUEPT Mock Claude: Cognitive Dissonance and Attitude Change
transcript:
[Introduction: Why Do We Change Our Minds?]
Today's lecture focuses on one of the most influential and extensively studied concepts in social psychology: cognitive dissonance and how it drives attitude change. The fundamental question we will explore is deceptively simple — why do people sometimes change what they believe, not because of new information, but because of what they have already done? If you think carefully about your own experience, you will almost certainly recognise this pattern. Perhaps you have taken a job you were not sure about and then, within weeks, found yourself defending it enthusiastically to friends who questioned your choice. Or perhaps you have made a purchase you later regretted, and found yourself spending considerable mental energy justifying the decision rather than admitting the mistake. These everyday experiences are precisely what the theory of cognitive dissonance sets out to explain. Before we move into the theory itself, it is worth appreciating why this topic matters beyond the level of personal anecdote. Attitude change is central to some of the most important questions in applied psychology — how do we encourage healthier behaviour, how do organisations shift workplace culture, and how do political movements persuade people to revise long-held views? Dissonance theory has shaped thinking in all of these areas, and understanding it gives us a window into the mechanisms that drive human motivation more broadly.

[Festinger's Theory of Cognitive Dissonance]
The theory was introduced by Leon Festinger in 1957. Festinger proposed that psychological discomfort — which he called cognitive dissonance — arises whenever a person holds two inconsistent beliefs simultaneously, or when a belief conflicts with a behaviour. The key insight was that people are strongly motivated to reduce this discomfort, and in doing so, they often change their attitudes rather than their behaviour. Festinger used the term cognition broadly to refer to any piece of knowledge, belief, or attitude — including knowledge about one's own actions. If I know that I smoke, and I also know that smoking causes cancer, these two cognitions are dissonant with each other. I feel uncomfortable, and I am motivated to resolve that discomfort in some way. Importantly, Festinger argued that the magnitude of the dissonance — and therefore the strength of the motivation to reduce it — depends on two factors: the importance of the conflicting cognitions, and the proportion of cognitions that are dissonant relative to those that are consonant. A minor inconsistency about something trivial produces little dissonance; a major inconsistency about something central to one's identity produces a great deal. This is why the theory has such broad applicability — from the mundane choices of everyday life to the most fundamental questions of moral self-image.

[The $1/$20 Experiment]
The most famous empirical demonstration of dissonance comes from an experiment conducted by Festinger and Carlsmith in 1959. Participants were first made to perform an extremely boring, repetitive task for an hour. They were then asked to tell the next participant — who was, in reality, a confederate of the experimenters — that the task had been interesting and enjoyable. Crucially, different participants were offered different payments for delivering this false message: some received one dollar, while others received twenty dollars. After delivering their positive endorsement of the task, all participants were asked by an independent interviewer how much they had genuinely enjoyed the task. The results were striking and, at first glance, counterintuitive. Those paid only one dollar rated the task significantly more enjoyable than those paid twenty dollars. Common sense would suggest the opposite — that a larger reward should produce more positive feelings. Dissonance theory, however, predicts exactly the opposite pattern. The participants who accepted only one dollar had to justify accepting so little money, and the most available way to do that was to persuade themselves that the task had actually been worthwhile. In other words, they changed their attitude to make it consistent with their behaviour. The participants who received twenty dollars had a ready-made external justification for saying something they did not believe — the money — and so they experienced little dissonance and felt no need to revise their opinion of the task. This experiment elegantly demonstrated that the relationship between reward and attitude change is far more complex than behaviourist learning theory had assumed.

[Strategies for Reducing Dissonance]
Psychologists have identified three primary strategies people use to reduce dissonance. The first is to change one of the conflicting cognitions — either the belief or the behaviour. The second is to add a new cognition that reconciles the conflict — to find a reason why the two seemingly contradictory things are not actually incompatible. The third is to reduce the perceived importance of one of the cognitions — to tell yourself that the contradiction does not really matter very much. Research consistently suggests that changing the behaviour or changing the belief is the most psychologically demanding of these strategies, because it requires individuals to act against established habits or to revise convictions that may be deeply embedded in their sense of self. This is precisely why the second and third strategies are so common — they allow the individual to maintain the existing belief and behaviour while still achieving a reduction in psychological discomfort. A classic real-world example involves smokers. Despite widespread public awareness of the serious health risks associated with smoking, many people who smoke continue to do so. Rather than quitting — which would be the most direct and complete resolution of the dissonance — they frequently reduce it by adding new cognitions, minimising the risk, or engaging in denial. For instance, a smoker might reason that the risks are overstated by the media, that exercising regularly offsets the damage, that a relative who smoked lived to ninety, or simply that life involves many risks and this one is no worse than others. Each of these cognitions reduces dissonance without requiring any change in behaviour, which is why they are so psychologically appealing even when they are logically weak.

[Bem's Self-Perception Theory: An Alternative Account]
Not all researchers accepted Festinger's account of what happens when beliefs and actions conflict. Daryl Bem proposed an alternative in the 1960s known as self-perception theory, which challenged the claim that dissonance involves genuine psychological arousal. According to Bem, people do not actually experience uncomfortable tension when their cognitions are inconsistent. Instead, they simply observe their own behaviour and draw inferences about their attitudes from it, in much the same way that an outside observer would. If I gave a speech favouring a position I did not hold, Bem would say I conclude I must hold it to some degree — not because I am reducing tension, but because my behaviour is my most available source of information about my own attitudes. The debate between Festinger's dissonance account and Bem's self-perception account generated a great deal of research. The current consensus among social psychologists is that both processes operate, but in different circumstances. Self-perception processes tend to dominate when attitudes are weak or when the individual has little prior commitment to a position. Dissonance processes, involving genuine arousal and motivated reasoning, tend to dominate when the behaviour contradicts a strongly held attitude, particularly one that is central to the person's self-concept.

[Aronson's Revision: The Centrality of the Self-Concept]
Elliot Aronson further developed dissonance theory by identifying what he believed was the true source of the motivational force. Aronson argued that dissonance is not simply about logical inconsistency between any two cognitions — it is specifically about behaviour that contradicts or threatens a person's self-concept. In Aronson's view, what makes dissonance powerful is that people care deeply about seeing themselves as competent, honest, and morally good. When behaviour violates those self-perceptions — when we act stupidly, dishonestly, or unkindly — we experience dissonance not merely because two beliefs clash, but because the very image we hold of ourselves is under threat. This revision helps explain why dissonance effects are strongest in contexts that involve moral self-evaluation, and why the emotions associated with dissonance — shame, guilt, embarrassment — have such a distinctive quality. It also has practical implications: people who have strong, positive self-images may actually experience more dissonance when they behave badly than people with lower self-esteem, because the gap between behaviour and self-concept is greater.

[Effort Justification]
A fascinating sub-phenomenon closely related to dissonance is what researchers call effort justification. This refers to the tendency for people to value an outcome more highly after working hard to achieve it. The logic is straightforward in dissonance terms: if I have invested significant time, energy, or discomfort in pursuing something, and that thing turns out to be mediocre, I face a dissonance between the effort I invested and the value of the outcome. To resolve this, I am motivated to perceive the outcome as more valuable than it actually is. Aronson and Mills conducted a now-classic study in 1959 in which participants underwent either a severe or a mild initiation procedure before joining what was described as a discussion group. Those who had undergone the severe initiation subsequently rated the group discussions — which were in fact deliberately made boring and trivial — as significantly more interesting and valuable than those who had undergone the mild initiation. The practical implications of effort justification are considerable. It helps explain why people who have worked hard to enter a profession, an institution, or a relationship may feel stronger loyalty to it than is objectively warranted — and why they may resist evidence that their effort was misplaced.

[Dissonance vs. Persuasion: Durability of Attitude Change]
A critical question for applied psychology is whether attitude change driven by dissonance is more durable than change produced by conventional persuasion. Research consistently shows that it is, and the reason comes down to personal commitment and active choice. When people freely choose a behaviour that contradicts their prior beliefs, they experience genuine dissonance, and the attitude change they make to resolve it feels self-generated — it is an adjustment they have made themselves, not an opinion they have been pressured into adopting. Persuasion, by contrast, can be mentally labelled as external influence. Once the persuader is absent, the attitude may revert, because it was never truly internalised. This distinction has important implications for health campaigns, educational programmes, and organisational change initiatives. In each domain, approaches that give individuals genuine agency — asking them to make a public commitment, to role-play a new behaviour, or to generate their own arguments for change — tend to produce deeper and more lasting shifts than even the most logically compelling arguments delivered from the outside. Understanding dissonance theory, in this sense, is not merely an academic exercise: it offers a genuinely practical framework for designing more effective interventions wherever changing minds and changing behaviour matter.

questions.q: According to Festinger, cognitive dissonance arises from holding two ___ beliefs simultaneously.
In Festinger and Carlsmith's experiment, why did participants paid $1 rate the task as more enjoyable than those paid $20?
answer: they had to justify accepting so little money
questions.explain: They changed their attitude to match their behaviour.

questions.q: What term describes the mental discomfort produced by conflicting beliefs or actions?
Which method of reducing dissonance is considered the most psychologically demanding? (Write one.)
questions.explain: Either accepted; both require significant effort.

questions.q: According to Bem's self-perception theory, how do people determine their own attitudes?
Smokers who continue despite knowing the health risks reduce dissonance primarily through what strategy? (Write one.)
questions.explain: Any one of the three accepted.

questions.q: Elliot Aronson revised dissonance theory by arguing that dissonance is strongest when behaviour threatens a person's ___.
Which factor makes attitude change through dissonance more durable than change through persuasion?
What is the technical term for the phenomenon where people value an outcome more highly after working hard to achieve it?

title: BUEPT Mock Claude: Social Stratification and Mobility
transcript:
[Introduction: Understanding Inequality]
One of the most fundamental concerns of sociology is understanding inequality — why some people have more wealth, prestige, and power than others, and how these differences are reproduced across generations rather than dissolving as societies modernise and expand. This is not a marginal question. It goes to the heart of how we evaluate the fairness of the societies we live in and the degree to which those societies live up to their own proclaimed ideals. Almost every modern society claims, at some level, to be a meritocracy — a system in which people rise and fall according to their talents, efforts, and achievements rather than the circumstances of their birth. Whether this claim is accurate — and to what extent — is precisely what the sociology of stratification sets out to examine. Today's lecture will introduce the core concepts and theoretical frameworks used in this field, examine the evidence on mobility across several countries, and end with a discussion of what recent data tells us about the current state of opportunity in advanced economies.

[Defining Social Stratification]
The starting point for this discussion is the concept of social stratification, which refers to the hierarchical arrangement of individuals and groups in a society based on factors such as wealth, occupation, education, and social prestige. Stratification is not a random or temporary outcome — it is a structural feature of societies, shaped by history, economics, law, and cultural norms. Sociologists distinguish stratification from simple inequality in a specific way: stratification refers to inequality that is systematic, that is recognised and legitimated by social institutions, and that is passed on across generations. A society in which outcomes varied randomly from person to person would be unequal but not stratified. What makes stratification distinctive is its persistence and its embeddedness in the fabric of social life. Understanding stratification therefore requires looking not just at who has more and who has less, but at the mechanisms — economic, cultural, and institutional — through which advantaged positions are maintained and transmitted.

[Marx: Class and the Means of Production]
The classical debate on stratification begins with Karl Marx. For Marx, the fundamental basis of class divisions in capitalist societies is ownership of the means of production. Those who own factories, land, machinery, and capital — the bourgeoisie — are able to extract surplus value from those who own nothing but their capacity to labour — the proletariat. In Marx's framework, this economic relationship is the foundation upon which all other social distinctions rest. The legal system, the state, dominant cultural values, and even prevailing ideas about morality and justice are, in Marx's view, shaped by the interests of the ruling class. Historical change, in this account, is driven by the fundamental antagonism between classes: as capitalism develops, the proletariat grows larger, its conditions worsen, and eventually it overthrows the existing order. While Marx's predictions about inevitable revolution have not been borne out in the form he anticipated, his insistence that economic relationships structure social life in profound ways continues to inform sociological analysis, even among scholars who reject the more deterministic elements of his framework.

[Weber: Three Dimensions of Stratification]
Max Weber offered a more nuanced and multidimensional account of stratification. Unlike Marx, who reduced stratification to a single economic axis, Weber argued that it operates across three distinct but interrelated dimensions: class, status, and power. Class, for Weber, refers to economic position — specifically, to one's market situation, which determines life chances and access to goods and opportunities. Status refers to social honour and prestige, which may correlate with economic position but need not. A successful criminal may be wealthy but low in status; a member of an old aristocratic family may have high status but modest income. Power, which Weber associated with political parties and organised groups, refers to the ability to achieve goals even against resistance from others. Weber's three-dimensional framework is more flexible than Marx's and better able to account for the complexity of stratification in modern societies. It helps explain, for instance, why professionals such as doctors and lawyers occupy a distinctive social position that cannot be fully captured by reference to their economic position alone — their status and their cultural authority are at least as important as their earnings in defining where they sit in the social hierarchy.

[Types of Social Mobility]
A central question in the study of stratification is how open or closed a society's hierarchy is — that is, how much movement between levels is possible and how frequently it actually occurs. Sociologists distinguish between different forms of mobility. Movement that occurs across generations — a child achieving a higher or lower occupational status than her parent — is called intergenerational mobility. By contrast, movement between different levels of the social hierarchy within a person's own lifetime — moving from a low-skilled manual job to a professional position, for example — is referred to as intragenerational mobility. Sociologists also distinguish between absolute and relative mobility. Absolute mobility refers to the overall amount of movement in a society — how many people end up in a different position from where they started. Relative mobility refers to the chances of people from different origins ending up in the same position — in other words, how fair the competition is. A society can have high absolute mobility if economic growth creates new higher-status positions, even if relative mobility — the fairness of the competition for those positions — remains low. This distinction is crucial for evaluating claims about meritocracy: a rising tide that lifts all boats may increase absolute mobility without making the competition for the best positions any more equal.

[Caste Systems and Ascription]
Not all stratification systems allow for mobility. In caste systems, social position is determined by birth. An individual is assigned at birth to a specific caste, which traditionally dictates occupation, marriage partners, ritual purity, and social interactions. Movement out of one's caste was, in traditional systems, socially impossible and often ritually prohibited — a person born into a low-status caste was expected to remain in it for life, and the children of that person would inherit the same position. The caste system historically associated with Hindu society in South Asia is the most widely studied example, but similar ascriptive systems have existed in many societies across history, including in feudal Europe, in the pre-Civil War American South, and in the apartheid system in South Africa. Class societies, by contrast, are theoretically open — positions are achieved rather than ascribed, meaning that individuals can in principle move up or down the hierarchy through their own efforts. Whether this theoretical openness translates into real opportunity is, of course, a different and more contested question, and one that empirical research has addressed extensively.

[Blau and Duncan: The American Occupational Structure]
One of the most influential empirical studies of social mobility in the United States was conducted by Peter Blau and Otis Dudley Duncan and published in 1967 under the title The American Occupational Structure. Their path-analysis model examined which factors best predicted a man's occupational attainment, using a large nationally representative sample. The study considered a range of predictors, including the respondent's own education, his first job, and his father's occupational status. While education and first job were both found to matter, the study found that father's occupational status remained the strongest single predictor of a son's eventual occupational position. This finding directly challenged the optimistic narrative of American meritocracy — the belief that in the United States, talent and hard work are what determine where you end up. Blau and Duncan's work sparked decades of subsequent research into the mechanisms through which class advantage is transmitted: through differential access to education, through social networks, through the cultural knowledge and behavioural dispositions that different families transmit to their children, and through the direct inheritance of economic resources.

[Bourdieu and Cultural Capital]
The French sociologist Pierre Bourdieu introduced a set of concepts that have become indispensable for understanding how class advantages are reproduced through cultural rather than purely economic mechanisms. The most important of these is cultural capital, which encompasses educational credentials, cultural knowledge, and the tastes and values that are recognised and rewarded by dominant social institutions — above all, by the educational system. Bourdieu argued that children from privileged class backgrounds arrive at school already possessing the linguistic codes, the aesthetic sensibilities, the familiarity with institutional expectations, and the general cultural ease that teachers and examiners reward — not because these children are inherently more able, but because their upbringing has given them a form of cultural capital that matches what the school values. Children from working-class backgrounds, by contrast, may possess different forms of cultural knowledge that are equally sophisticated in their own context, but that are not recognised or rewarded within the educational system. Through this process, which Bourdieu called social reproduction, the educational system appears to operate on the basis of merit and ability while actually encoding and perpetuating class advantage. The educational credentials that result from this process then confer occupational advantages, completing the cycle.

[Contemporary Trends in Social Mobility]
What does the evidence tell us about mobility in advanced economies today? Multiple large-scale longitudinal studies conducted in the United States, the United Kingdom, Scandinavia, and elsewhere converge on an unsettling finding: social mobility rates have declined significantly over the past four decades. Research by economist Raj Chetty and his colleagues at Harvard has shown that in the United States, the probability that a child born into the bottom income quintile will reach the top quintile as an adult has fallen substantially compared to previous generations. Similar findings have been reported in the United Kingdom, where the link between a father's income and a son's eventual income has remained stubbornly strong despite decades of educational expansion. These trends reflect a convergence of structural factors: rising wealth concentration at the top, increasing housing costs in high-opportunity cities, credential inflation that has raised the price of entry to professional careers, and the growing importance of social networks and cultural capital in a knowledge-based economy. The sociological consensus is that while formal legal barriers to mobility — based on race, gender, or caste — have largely been dismantled in advanced democracies, the structural and economic barriers to moving between classes have in many respects grown stronger. The meritocratic ideal is therefore not simply unrealised; there is evidence that it is becoming harder to achieve, even as it continues to be the dominant framework through which people in these societies understand their own life prospects.

questions.q: What term do sociologists use to describe the hierarchical arrangement of individuals and groups in a society?
According to Marx, what is the fundamental basis of class divisions in capitalist societies?
answer: ownership of the means of production
questions.explain: Marx: bourgeoisie owns; proletariat does not.

questions.q: Unlike Marx, Weber argued that social stratification is determined by three dimensions. Name one.
questions.explain: Weber's three-component model; any one accepted.

questions.q: What term describes movement between different levels of the social hierarchy within a person's own lifetime?
According to Blau and Duncan's study, which factor was the strongest predictor of occupational status in the United States?
In caste systems, social position is determined by ___.
What does Bourdieu's concept of 'cultural capital' refer to? (Write one aspect.)
Recent studies on social mobility in advanced economies show that mobility rates have ___ over the past four decades.

title: BUEPT Mock Claude: Market Failures and Government Intervention
transcript:
[Introduction: When Markets Do Not Work]
A central assumption of classical economics is that competitive markets, when left to operate freely, produce efficient outcomes. They allocate resources to their most valued uses, translate dispersed individual knowledge into prices, and coordinate the behaviour of millions of people without any central authority directing them. This proposition — associated above all with Adam Smith's metaphor of the invisible hand — forms the theoretical foundation for market-based economies and provides the intellectual basis for the presumption in favour of limited government intervention. And in many contexts, it holds up well. Markets for consumer goods, for financial instruments, and for many categories of labour do appear to function with impressive efficiency under the right conditions. Yet real-world markets frequently and systematically deviate from this ideal in ways that have important consequences for social welfare. Today's lecture is concerned with those deviations. They are collectively described by the term market failure, which refers to any situation in which markets, operating without intervention, fail to allocate resources efficiently. Understanding market failures is essential not only for economic theory but for evaluating the practical case for government intervention in specific sectors and contexts.

[Externalities: Costs and Benefits Beyond the Transaction]
The first major category of market failure is externalities — costs or benefits that fall on third parties who are not directly involved in a market transaction and therefore not reflected in the prices that guide market behaviour. When a chemical plant discharges effluent into a river, the economic costs of that pollution are borne not by the plant or by the customers who buy its products, but by communities, fishers, and ecosystems downstream. Because these costs are external to the firm's profit-and-loss calculation, the firm has no market incentive to take them into account, and will therefore produce more than is socially optimal. The problem was identified clearly by the British economist Arthur Pigou in the early twentieth century. Pigou argued that the most effective government response to a negative externality is to impose a corrective tax equal to the marginal external cost — what economists now refer to as a Pigouvian tax. By raising the firm's private costs to reflect the true social costs of its production, such a tax gives the firm an incentive to reduce output to the socially efficient level. Positive externalities — benefits that spill over to third parties — create the mirror-image problem: because producers cannot capture all the benefits they generate, they tend to underproduce. Education is the standard example: a better-educated workforce generates benefits for employers, neighbours, and society at large that the individual student and her family cannot fully appropriate. The standard policy response to positive externalities is a subsidy rather than a tax.

[The Coase Theorem: Private Solutions to Externalities]
The case for government intervention in response to externalities was challenged forcefully by Ronald Coase in a landmark 1960 paper. Coase demonstrated that in many circumstances, the parties affected by an externality can resolve the problem among themselves through negotiation, without the need for government involvement. What economists now call the Coase Theorem states that when transaction costs are low and property rights are clearly defined, bargaining between affected parties will produce an efficient outcome regardless of how legal liability is initially assigned. To use a simple example: if a factory and a group of downstream residents have well-defined legal rights over the use of the river, and if they can negotiate costlessly, they will reach an agreement that internalises the externality — either the factory pays the residents for the right to pollute up to an agreed level, or the residents pay the factory to reduce its emissions. The critical limitation of the Coase Theorem is, of course, that its conditions are rarely met in practice. Transaction costs are rarely low enough, and property rights over shared resources like air and water are rarely defined clearly enough, to allow private negotiation to work at scale. This is particularly true for diffuse, large-scale environmental problems like climate change, where millions of parties are involved and the scientific linkages between individual actions and outcomes are complex and contested.

[Public Goods: Non-Exclusion and Non-Rivalry]
A second major source of market failure arises from a specific category of goods that private markets are structurally incapable of providing efficiently. These are public goods, which are characterised by two properties that distinguish them from the private goods that markets handle well. They are non-excludable and non-rival. Non-excludability means that once the good has been provided, it is impossible or prohibitively costly to prevent anyone from benefiting from it. Non-rivalry means that one person's consumption of the good does not diminish its availability to anyone else. National defence is the textbook example: the protection it provides extends to all residents of a country simultaneously, and one person's enjoyment of that protection does not reduce the protection available to any other resident. Other examples include clean air in a city, lighthouse signals, basic scientific research, and public health infrastructure such as disease surveillance systems. Because private firms cannot charge for non-excludable goods — those who refuse to pay cannot be excluded from benefiting — they have no financial incentive to produce them. The predictable result is a phenomenon known as the free-rider problem: individuals benefit from the public good while expecting others to bear the costs of providing it. In equilibrium, the good is underprovided or not provided at all by the private market, which is why government provision or mandatory collective funding is typically necessary.

[Asymmetric Information and Market Breakdown]
A third form of market failure involves information problems. Standard economic theory assumes that buyers and sellers are equally and fully informed — that both parties to a transaction know the quality of what is being exchanged and the terms on which the exchange is taking place. In reality, this assumption is routinely violated. One party — typically the seller — frequently knows considerably more about the good being sold than the buyer does. This situation is described by the term asymmetric information. Its consequences can be severe, as demonstrated with great elegance by George Akerlof in his celebrated 1970 paper, 'The Market for Lemons: Quality Uncertainty and the Market Mechanism.' Akerlof used the market for second-hand cars as his central example. In American slang, a defective car is called a 'lemon.' Sellers of used cars know whether their particular car is a lemon or a high-quality vehicle, but prospective buyers typically cannot tell the difference before purchase. Rational buyers, aware of this uncertainty, are therefore only willing to pay a price that reflects the average quality of cars on the market. But this average price is too low for owners of high-quality cars to accept — they withdraw their cars from the market rather than sell below their true value. As high-quality cars exit the market, the average quality of cars available for sale falls. This in turn causes rational buyers to lower their willingness to pay further, which drives out yet more good cars in a self-reinforcing downward spiral — a process economists call adverse selection. In the most extreme case, the market collapses entirely: only lemons are traded, at a price that reflects their low quality, even though there would have been mutually beneficial trades available if both parties had been equally informed. Akerlof's model has been applied to markets far beyond used cars, including health insurance, credit markets, and labour markets — wherever the quality of what is being sold is difficult for the buyer to assess before the transaction is complete.

[Price Controls as a Policy Instrument]
Governments respond to market failures through a range of policy instruments, and one of the most direct and politically visible is the imposition of price controls — legal limits on the prices that can be charged in particular markets. There are two basic forms. A price ceiling sets a maximum price below the equilibrium market price. If effective, it prevents the price from rising to the level that would balance supply and demand, typically resulting in a shortage — the quantity demanded at the controlled price exceeds the quantity supplied. Rent control in urban housing markets is the most widely studied and debated example. A price floor — such as the minimum wage — sets a minimum price above the equilibrium level. If effective, it prevents the price from falling to the market-clearing level, typically resulting in a surplus — the quantity supplied exceeds the quantity demanded. In the case of the minimum wage, this means that the supply of labour at the minimum wage price may exceed the demand for it, which is the mechanism through which critics argue that minimum wages cause unemployment, though the empirical evidence on this is considerably more complex than the simple model suggests. While these interventions can address important social concerns — such as housing affordability or poverty wages — economists caution that they generate distortions that must be carefully evaluated against their intended benefits.

[Evaluating Intervention: The Cost-Benefit Framework]
How should economists and policymakers decide whether government intervention in response to a market failure is warranted? The normative standard used in welfare economics is a cost-benefit framework: intervention is justified when the social gains it produces — measured as the improvement in allocative efficiency plus any distributional benefits — exceed the costs it imposes, including administrative costs, unintended distortions, compliance costs, and the efficiency losses that typically accompany taxation. The key principle is that the costs of intervention must always be weighed against the market failure or inefficiency that the intervention is designed to correct. Government action is not automatically superior to an imperfect market — in some cases, particularly when the market failure is minor or when the available policy instruments are blunt, intervention may leave society worse off than doing nothing at all. This is what economists mean when they refer to government failure: the tendency for well-intentioned policies to produce inefficiencies of their own, through misaligned incentives, regulatory capture, information problems facing policymakers, or political pressures that distort the design and implementation of policy. The lesson that decades of studying both market failures and government failures teaches us is that the relevant comparison is never between a perfect market and an imperfect government, or between a perfect government and an imperfect market. It is always between realistic, imperfect alternatives — and making that comparison well requires both the analytical tools of economics and the institutional knowledge needed to understand how policies actually operate in practice.

questions.q: What term describes a situation in which markets fail to allocate resources efficiently on their own?
According to Pigou, what mechanism should governments use to correct for negative externalities?
questions.explain: 'Tax equal to the marginal social cost' also accepted.

questions.q: What distinguishes a public good from a private good? Name one characteristic.
questions.explain: Either property accepted; both define public goods.

questions.q: The problem that arises when individuals benefit from a public good without contributing to its cost is called ___.
According to Coase, under what condition can private parties resolve externality problems without government intervention?
questions.explain: Either condition from the Coase Theorem accepted.

questions.q: What term describes the situation where one party in a transaction has more information than the other?
questions.explain: Broad term covering adverse selection and moral hazard.

questions.q: In Akerlof's used car market model, what happens to the average quality of cars as a result of asymmetric information?
When evaluating intervention, economists argue that its costs must be weighed against the ___ it is designed to correct.
questions.explain: Standard cost-benefit framework for policy evaluation.

questions.q: Which form of government intervention involves setting maximum or minimum prices in a market? (Write one example.)
